segments.text: Start back in public session this afternoon on the
planning bill and Natural Environment Bill.
And we have our first submitter with us,
the Civil Contractors New Zealand.
Uh, welcome to the select committee this afternoon.
It's great to have you with us.

to you for your opening remarks and introductions

for allowing us a little bit more time,

that independent consultant,

a management system that becomes frankly unwieldy, uh,
over litigious, incredibly falsely

and the communities we serve.

for infrastructure delivery.

are not optional, not necessary
for the benefit of our communities.
So our core message really is that in the consenting
and compliance framework, we have to be practical, we have
to be proportionate, and it has to be workable.
If, uh, we are serious about delivering infrastructure
efficiently and affordably to do this
and to better protect the environment, we have
to reduce complexity.
And we've got a right size the system.
We support the efforts of the bill
to reduce unnecessary consenting for routine civil works
and to promote greater standardization across regions.
These are foundational steps to unlocking housing,
unlocking regional growth, and eventually productivity.
We do, however, strongly caution against any reform
that simply replaces ambiguities with more ambiguity.

in truth, how its application will come into force
